segments.s433-g145: This is an imperative clause: نگه کن is a two-word command ('look' / 'pay attention') with نگه (a noun/verb stem 'look, keep') plus the auxiliary imperative کن. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'), and چون here functions temporally ('when/as') governing the following clause 'او شود تاجدار' — او (he) is the subject and شود (3rd sing. present/subjunctive of شدن) means 'becomes', followed by تاجدار 'crowned/wearer of the crown'.
segments.s433-g146: به پیش آورد is a verbal phrase 'bring before / present': به + پیش (toward/in the presence of) + آورد (past/stem 'bring'). کین is the object meaning 'hatred/vengeance' and اسفندیار is the possessor/name; word order is object after the verb as typical in poetic Persian (here the phrase means 'will bring Esfandiyar's grudge before [someone]').
segments.s433-g147: بدو (to him) is a dative/indirect-object particle 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت here functions as 'said' with the speaker name رستم following it by topical position (Rostam). که introduces the content clause. با آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'with heaven' (آسمان = 'heaven'); word order reflects the older/poetic placement of phrases.
segments.s433-g148: نتابد is a negative present/optative use of تابیدن here meaning 'to befit / to be tolerated' (نتابد = 'does not befit / does not tolerate'); بداندیش is an adjective/noun 'ill‑minded, malicious'. و links the two coordinated nouns/phrases; نیکی گمان is a genitive-like juxtaposition 'suspicion/doubt of goodness' (گمان here = 'suspicion'). The whole clause states that heaven does not approve of malice and suspicion of good.
segments.s433-g149: This clause begins with the subject pronoun من 'I' followed by آن 'that/that one' as the direct object of برگزیدم (past 'I chose'). که introduces a purpose/result clause: چشم خرد 'the eye of wisdom' — چشم (here 'eye' in the figurative sense of sight or regard) followed by خرد ('wisdom'). Word order is typical older Persian: subject — object — verb — relative/purpose clause.
segments.s433-g150: بدو is again dative 'to him'; بنگرد is a present/subjunctive form of نگریستن 'to look, to regard' (here purpose/subjunctive: 'may look upon him'). نام یاد آورد is a verbal phrase 'bring the name to remembrance' (یاد آورد = 'recall/bring to mind' with نام 'name' as its object). The infinitive/ subjunctive sequence expresses the intended result of the choosing.
segments.s433-g151: گر is the conditional 'if'. او is the subject 'he'. بد کند is a verb phrase 'does evil' (بد = 'bad', کند = 'does/makes'). پیچد از روزگار uses پیچیدن 'to turn' with از روزگار ('from fate/time') — here روزگار functions as 'fate'; the sense is 'if fate turns [against him]'. The clause is conditional, setting the circumstance for the following admonition.
segments.s433-g152: This is the admonitory main clause addressed to تو 'you'. چشم بلا را is a marked object ('the eye of calamity' with را the definite object marker). به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste / hastily'. مخار is an imperative/negative-imperative form in the poem meaning roughly 'do not cast/aim' (poetic/archaic); combined, the clause says 'do not hastily direct the eye of calamity at him.'
segments.s433-g137: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The clause سر برکشد is literal '(he) lifts (his) head' with the noun سر before the verb برکشد (classical word order). زود is an adverb modifying جوید; جوید is the present-tense verb 'seeks' and شکار is its object 'prey'.
segments.s433-g138: نخست is an adverb 'first'. اندر آید is inverted classical order meaning 'comes/enters in' (اندر before the verb). به پروردگار marks direction/destination 'to the one who reared him' (پروردگار here means 'nurturer' rather than the divine Lord).
segments.s433-g139: دو پهلو is a compound meaning 'the two flanks/sides'. برآشفته is a past-participial/adjectival form 'disturbed, agitated' modifying پهلو. از introduces the cause 'from/because of' and خشم بد is 'bad/violent anger' (بد intensifies خشم).
segments.s433-g140: نخستین 'the first' qualifies 'ازان بد' ('of that evil/misfortune'). به زابل رسد literally 'reaches Zabul'; here رسد carries the sense 'befalls/comes to' (a misfortune befalls Zabul).
segments.s433-g141: The initial چو is temporal 'when'. شد functions as an auxiliary here ('was' / perfective formation). کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain'. شاهی is an epithet 'royal' (qualifying the following name), and the second چو is the classical 'as/namely' introducing the name اسفندیار—together: 'when the royal one, Esfandiyar, was slain.'
segments.s433-g142: ببینند is the 3rd-person plural present/historical-present 'they see/behold' used to report the outcome. ازین پس is a fixed adverbial phrase 'from this on / thereafter' (از + این in archaic spelling ین + پس). بد روزگار is a nominal phrase 'bad time(s)' = 'ill days / misfortune.'
segments.s433-g143: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بهمن here is a proper name (a person/figure) rather than the common noun. رسد بد is an impersonal construction 'a misfortune befalls' or 'evil reaches', and به زابلستان marks the recipient 'to Zabulistan'.
segments.s433-g144: بپیچند is a 3rd-person plural present/formal-subjunctive form of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- and here means 'are thrown into confusion/are turned' (verb before subject in inverted poetic order). پیران کابلستان is the subject 'the elders of Kabulistan'.
segments.s433-g81: This clause is temporal/subordinate: 'به ایران' (to/in Iran) sets the place, 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when', and the verb phrase 'دین بهی راست شد' means 'the good religion became established'. 'راست شد' is a poetic/perfective phrasing meaning 'was made right/established' (شد = became/happened). Word order places the place phrase before the temporal 'چو' + clause, typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s433-g82: This is the main clause. 'بزرگی و شاهی' is the compound subject/thing referenced; 'م' appears here but does not match the listed sense entries (it functions as the enclitic object 'مرا' or similar in the original, i.e. marking the person concerned). 'را' is the object/definite marker attached after that; 'خواست شد' is a compound indicating 'was wished/desired' (خواسـت in a passive-like construction with شد). The overall syntax is passive/impersonal: 'greatness and kingship were sought for me.'
segments.s433-g83: 'به پیش سران' = 'before/in the presence of the chiefs' (پیش here = 'in the presence of'); 'پندها' is the direct object 'admonitions/advices'; 'دادیم' is the past first-person plural 'we gave/issued'. Word order is typical Persian: indirect/location phrase, subject omitted (we), object, verb at end.
segments.s433-g84: Adverbial 'نهانی' (secretly) opens the clause; 'به کشتن' is a purposive infinitival phrase literally 'to killing' meaning 'for killing' or 'to be killed'; 'فرستادیم' is the past 'we sent/dispatched'. Together they mean 'we secretly sent [them] to be killed.' Note the supplied object is implied from context and omitted here.
segments.s433-g85: 'کنون' = 'now' sets the time. 'زین سخن' is an ablative/relative phrase 'from this speech' (here 'زین' is the demonstrative 'from this'), 'یافتی' is second person past/perfect 'you have found/obtained', and 'کام دل' is the object 'the desire/fulfillment of the heart'. The verb comes after the phrase, typical Persian order; note 'زین' as used here is a poetic demonstrative and did not match the provided nominal senses.
segments.s433-g86: Two imperatives joined by 'و': 'بیارای' is an imperative meaning 'adorn/prepare (yourself)' or 'make ready', and 'بنشین' is the imperative 'sit down'. The following prepositional phrase 'به آرام دل' ('with a calm heart') specifies the manner. Note that some listed lexical senses did not reflect the verb imperative reading in the text, so the index for 'بیارای' is marked accordingly.
segments.s433-g87: 'چو' = 'when'; 'ایمن شدی' is second-person perfective 'you became safe' (شدی = you became); 'مرگ را دور کن' is an imperative clause 'put/keep death at a distance' (مرگ را = death as definite object, دور کن = 'do away/drive away'). The structure is conditional/temporal leading to the command that follows.
segments.s433-g88: 'به ایوان شاهی' = 'to/at the royal hall' (ایوان شاهی = the king's/royal portico); 'یکی سور کن' is an imperative 'make one feast' — 'یکی' = 'one / a certain', 'سور' = 'feast/banquet', 'کن' = imperative 'do/make'. The clause issues the command to hold a banquet in the royal hall.
segments.s433-g33: بهانه تو بودی is literally 'you were (the) pretext' with بودی the 2nd-person past of 'to be'. 'پدر بد زمان' is an epithet in apposition to 'تو' (no ezafe is written because this is poetic diction) meaning 'father of bad times' or 'father of an ill era'. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis but the structure is subject (تو) + predicate (بهانه ... بودی) with an appositive noun phrase following.
segments.s433-g34: The repeated نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor' listing items that are absent or irrelevant; no verb is repeated and the final phrase تیر و کمان links two weapons with و 'and'. The entire segment is a nominal list functioning as what is lacking or not present.
segments.s433-g35: گفت مرا (here written مرا گفت) is the older/poetic word order for 'he said to me' with the object pronoun placed before the verb; رو is an imperative 'go' (or a verbal imperative meaning 'go!'), سیستان را is the definite object marked by را, and بسوز is the imperative 'burn!'. Together they report direct imperative speech: 'Go, burn Sistan.'
segments.s433-g36: نخواهم is first-person negative 'I will not want / I will not permit'; کزین پس combines کِ + زین + پس meaning 'from this onward / henceforth'; بود is the verb 'be' (here meaning 'there be' or 'exist'); نیمروز is the place-name Nimruz. The clause expresses the speaker's refusal that Nimruz exist after this point.
segments.s433-g37: بکوشید is an exhortation/subjunctive meaning 'strive' or 'make the effort' (it can be an imperative or optative in classical style); تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; لشکر و تاج و گنج is a coordinated list (army, crown, and treasure) acting as the objects of the purpose clause.
segments.s433-g38: بدو ماند literally 'remain to him' — بدو is the allative 'to him/unto him' and ماند is 'remain / be left', so 'remain with him'. The second clause و من بمانم uses و 'and' plus بمانم (1st-person subjunctive/present) 'that I remain' and به رنج 'in suffering' (به marking the state). Together: 'so that ... remain with him, and I remain in hardship.'
segments.s433-g39: کنون is an adverb 'now' setting the temporal frame. The sequence بهمن این نامور پور من is appositional: the name 'Bahman' followed by the demonstrative + epithet 'this famed son of mine' (پور من = 'my son'). There is no linking copula; the nouns and modifiers are juxtaposed in typical poetic style.
segments.s433-g40: خردمند و بیدار are two adjectives 'wise and alert' that modify the following noun دستور (here functioning as 'commander' or 'one who gives orders') and من is the possessor 'my'. The adjective phrase precedes the noun for emphasis; together they form 'my wise and vigilant commander.'
segments.s433-g65: کنون is an adverb meaning “now.” نیک نامت is a two-word phrase ‘nīk nām‑et’ = “your good name/reputation” (نیک modifies نام and -ت is the 2nd‑person suffix on نام). به بد بازگشت is a verbal phrase: به + بد “to/into bad” and بازگشت is a past/preterite verbal form ‘returned/turned’ (third‑person sense: ‘has turned’). Word order is topical/poetic: temporal adverb → subject phrase → predicate.
segments.s433-g66: ز من is a causal/agentive phrase here: ز “because of/from” + من “me.” روی گیتی is a compound image—روی “face/surface” + گیتی “the world”—i.e. ‘the face of the world’ or ‘the world’s aspect.’ پرآواز گشت is a stative/resultative: پرآواز “noisy/renowned” (lit. ‘full‑voiced’) + گشت (become) = “became famed/was widely spoken of.”
segments.s433-g67: غم آمد is a simple past clause ‘sorrow came.’ روان ترا is the noun روان “soul/spirit” followed by enclitic ت (second‑person poss./object) and then the object marker را — a classical order equivalent to ‘your soul.’ بهره زین = ‘the share/portion from this’ (بهره = portion). The word زین here is written joined but functions as ‘from this/of this’ in context (see note about senses).
segments.s433-g68: چنین بود is a verbless/result clause ‘thus was’ (چنین = ‘thus/so’, بود = past of بودن ‘was’). رای here means ‘plan/counsel/decision’ (the divine plan), and جهان‌آفرین is a compounded title ‘World‑Creator’ (creator of the world). The whole line reads as a statement that what happened was the decree/counsel of the Creator.
segments.s433-g69: چنین گفت پس is ‘thus he said then’ (چنین = thus, گفت = said; note: in the provided senses گفت was listed as ‘conversation’ but here functions as the verb ‘said’). با پشوتن = ‘to/with Peshotan’ (با + proper name; in classical usage با can introduce the interlocutor). که introduces the content clause (‘that/that is’).
segments.s433-g70: نجویم همی is the speaker’s negated present/factual statement: ن‑ (negative) + جویم (seek, 1st person) with the habitual/emphatic particle همی; literal ‘I do not seek / I will not seek.’ زین here = ‘from this’ (ز + این) and جهان = ‘the world’; جز = ‘except/other than’; کفن = ‘shroud.’ Word order is typical for poetic Persian: verb phrase before the prepositional/partitive phrase.
segments.s433-g71: چو من بگذرم is a temporal/subordinate clause: چو = ‘when/if’, من = ‘I’, بگذرم = first‑person present/past subjunctive form of گذشتن ‘to pass’ meaning ‘when I pass/when I am gone.’ زین here functions as ‘from this’ (z‑in = ‘from/of this’). سپنجی is an adjective ‘spongy’ (سپنج = sponge; used metaphorically for the body), and سرای in context means ‘house/abode’ (an older usage ‘house’ rather than the provided senses about composing).
segments.s433-g72: تو لشکر بیارای is an imperative/optative instruction to the addressee: تو ‘you’ + لشکر ‘army’ + بیارای here functions as ‘array/prepare/muster’ (classical poetic imperative). و = ‘and’. شو is the 2nd‑person imperative of شدن ‘become/return’ (here ‘become/assume’) and باز جای = ‘again (باز) [to your] place/post’ (جای = place/position). The sequence gives two commands: assemble the troops and resume your place.
segments.s433-g121: The phrase is a compact past action: 'رستم' (Rostam) appears before the verb and is the agent. 'بر و جامه' lists outer garment(s) (poetic pairing) joined by 'و' (and). 'همی' is the classical narrative/progressive particle that intensifies or stretches the past action, and 'پاره کرد' is the compound past of 'پاره کردن' (to tear) where 'کرد' is the past auxiliary and 'پاره' the verbal stem.
segments.s433-g122: Two parallel nominal phrases describe states of Rostam using the possessive enclitic '-ش' (= his). 'سرش پر ز خاک' = 'his head full of dust' and 'دلش پر ز درد' = 'his heart full of pain'; 'پر' here means 'full (of)' and 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'of/from'. The construction omits any copula and uses noun + 'پر ز' + complement to express being filled with something.
segments.s433-g123: 'همی' again gives a continuous or emphatic flavour ('kept/was saying'). 'گفت' is the past 'said' (here rendered as he cried/speaking). 'زار‌ای' is a lamenting interjection/vocative ('O wretched one' / a cry of grief). 'نبرده سوار' literally 'the rider has not taken/carried (him)'; 'نبرده' is the negative perfective and 'سوار' is 'rider' — the phrase is likely an anguished exclamation about someone not being carried away by a rider (i.e. not saved).
segments.s433-g124: A string of nouns/titles in apposition or vocative use: 'نیا' (ancestor) + 'شاه' (king) + 'جنگی' (warlike/warrior) + 'پدر' (father) + 'شهریار' (sovereign). Poetic Persian often stacks epithets without overt linking particles; here they address or name the fallen with a catalogue of grand titles.
segments.s433-g125: 'به خوبی شده' = 'has become good/well' where 'به' makes an adverbial phrase ('well' / '-ly') and 'شده' is the perfect/passive participle 'has become'. 'در جهان نام من' places 'my name' in the locative 'in the world' (poetic inversion of word order emphasizes 'in the world'). Overall: 'my name has become (known) well in the world.'
segments.s433-g126: 'ز' here means 'because of' (poetic 'از'), introducing the cause. 'گشتاسپ' is the name Gushtasp. 'بد شد' = 'turned out badly' (بد = bad, شد = became). 'سرانجام من' = 'my outcome/end' (noun phrase with 'من' as possessor). The clause says that, owing to Gushtasp, his end was ill.
segments.s433-g127: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'بسیار' modifies the verb to mean 'greatly/much'. 'بگریست' is the simple past 'he wept'. 'با کشته گفت' uses 'با' to indicate the person he addresses or laments over ('to/with the slain') and 'گفت' is 'he said' — syntactically: 'When he wept much, he, addressing the slain, said...'.
segments.s433-g128: 'که‌ای' here functions like 'که ای' introducing a direct exclamation or rhetorical question ('who — O who'). 'در جهان شاه' = 'a king in the world' and 'بی‌یار و جفت' are predicative adjectives meaning 'without companion/friend and without a mate' (the copula is omitted in poetic Persian). Together they form the lament: 'Who in the world is a king without friend and mate?'.
segments.s433-g129: روان تو بادا میان بهشت is an optative clause. روان (noun) + تو (possessive) = “your soul”; بادا is an optative particle meaning “may it be.” میان بهشت is a locative phrase (“in the midst of Paradise”), with میان functioning as “mid/middle” placed after the predicate in the classical/poetic word order.
segments.s433-g130: بداندیش تو بدرود هرچ کشت is a wish against an enemy. بداندیش functions as “ill‑wisher/ill‑minded (one),” with تو as the possessive attached after it (“your ill‑wisher”). بدرود here expresses a wish for disappearance/perishing (“may ... be gone/ perish”). هرچ کشت = “everything he has slain/whatsoever he killed,” where کشت is used in the sense of killing; the agent (he/the ill‑wisher) is omitted but understood.
segments.s433-g131: زواره بدو گفت کای نامدار is a reported speech header. زواره is a proper name (the speaker); بدو = 'to him.' گفت is the verb 'said' (classical reportive). کای نامدار is a vocative: کای = 'O!' (poetic) and نامدار = 'renowned/illustrious.'
segments.s433-g132: نبایست پذرفت زو زینهار is a cautionary sentence. نبایست is a negative modal meaning 'ought not/should not'; پذرفت is the verbal root meaning 'to accept' (here used as the action one ought not do). ز (poetic از) + و (here part of the clitic combination) yields زو = 'from him' (i.e. 'accept from him'). زینهار is an interjection/warning 'beware!/take heed!'.
segments.s433-g133: ز دهقان تو نشنیدی آن داستان uses a poetic preposition z (= از) + agent to mark the source: 'from the دهقان (peasant/dihqān).' تو is the second‑person pronoun; نشنیدی is past (second singular) with negative prefix ن-: 'did you not hear?' آن داستان is the direct object 'that tale.' Word order in classical Persian can place the source before the verb.
segments.s433-g134: که یاد آرد از گفته باستان is a relative clause modifying 'that tale.' که = 'which/that.' یاد آرد is a two‑word predicate meaning 'brings to mind' (یاد = remembrance/memory, آرد = 'brings' — classical present form), so 'which recalls/reminds.' از + گفته = 'from/of what is said' (گفته here = 'saying/report'); باستان = 'ancient/of old' qualifying گفته.
segments.s433-g135: که‌گر پروری بچه نره‌شیر begins a conditional: که‌گر = 'if' (archaic/poetic). پروری is a deverbal noun meaning 'the raising/nurturing' (here functioning as the action 'if one raises'); بچه = 'young/offspring' (here 'cub'); نره‑شیر = compound 'male‑lion' (the animal whose cub is meant). Classical Persian often uses such nominal/verbal nouns to express 'if X is raised...'.
segments.s433-g136: شود تیزدندان و گردد دلیر contains two verbs of becoming. شود (شد) = 'becomes/will be' and گردد (گشت/گرد) = 'becomes' (a stylistic repetition). تیزدندان = 'sharp‑toothed' (predicate after شود). و = 'and.' دلیر = 'brave.' This yields 'it will become sharp‑toothed and become brave.'
segments.s433-g9: تهمتن is the subject (the epithet/name Tahmātan). به گفتار او literally marks the target of listening: به + گفتار ("to/for speech") with او specifying whose speech. داد گوش is a light-verb collocation (داد + گوش) meaning "gave ear" = "listened/paid attention"; داد is past tense "gave" used as a light verb and گوش here means "attention/ear."
segments.s433-g10: پیاده here functions as a noun meaning "foot-soldiers/infantry." بیامد is past tense "came" (he/they came). برش is the poetic contraction of بر + ش (on/upon him) — an archaic clitic meaning "upon him" (this specific usage is not present in the provided sense list). با introduces the manner/instrument "with," and خروش is the cry/roar accompanying the action.
segments.s433-g11: همی is the frequentive/progressive particle often used in epic Persian to indicate continued or habitual action (here: ongoing past). ریخت is the past of ریختن used intransitively "poured/fell." از دیدگان marks the source/point of origin "from the eyes," and آب گرم literally "warm water" is used for tears—warm (گرم) qualifying the water.
segments.s433-g12: همی again shows ongoing action. مویه is the noun/gerund "lament/woe," and کردش is the light verb کرد with the object clitic -ش ("him/it"), so مویه کردش = "made lament for him" / "lamented him." به here functions instrumentally/adverbially ("with/by"), آوای نرم = "a soft/gentle voice/melody," indicating the manner of the lament.
segments.s433-g13: چو here means "when" introducing a temporal clause. دستان (literally "hands") functions idiomatically as the agents who hear or carry news (retainers/messengers). خبر یافت is a common verbal phrase: یافت (past of یافتن, "found/learned/heard") with خبر as its object — "heard news" — and از رزمگاه marks the source of that news.
segments.s433-g14: ز (poetic z) = "from" and ایوان = "iwan/porch." چو here is a simile particle "like/as," باد = "wind" is the basis of the simile, اندر آمد = "entered/intruded" (came in), and به راه marks direction "onto/to the road." The whole phrase means he sallied forth from the porch and onto the road as if blown by the wind.
segments.s433-g15: ز = "from" (poetic). خانه is "house." بیامد is past "came." به introduces the destination and دشت نبرد is a compound notion "the plain of battle" (دشت qualified by نبرد). The verb is past and the phrase describes motion from the house toward the battlefield plain.
segments.s433-g16: دو دیده = "two eyes" (a poetic way to say "both eyes"). پر (after a noun) means "full (of)" and is followed by از + noun to indicate what they are full of: از آب = "of water" (tears). و links the two clauses; دل پر ز درد uses پر again with the poetic variant ز (from/of) to mark what the heart is full of: درد = sorrow/pain (emotional). Word order places the adjective-like پر after the noun with از/ز marking the content.
segments.s433-g97: کزو بازگردی به مادر بگوی packs several notions typical of classical Persian: کِزو (from whom / whence) precedes the verb بازگردی ('you return') and sets the origin or point of return; به مادر بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive clause 'say to the mother'. Grammatically, the phrase places the adverbial/relational element (کِزو) before the verb and then names the addressee (به مادر) followed by the verb (بگوی). Note: بگوی here is the subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن 'to tell/say' even though the listed lemma senses are nominal — functionally it acts as 'tell'.
segments.s433-g98: که سیر آمد از رزم پرخاشجوی is a reporting clause introduced by که 'that'. سیر آمد is a compound meaning 'came sated / came having had one's fill' (سیر = satiety used adjectivally, آمد = came). از رزم specifies the source/cause 'from the battle/war', and پرخاشجوی is a postpositive adjective modifying رزم ('the quarrelsome / aggressive fighting'). Word order is typical: verb (آمد) follows the predicate noun-adjective cluster.
segments.s433-g99: که here begins an explanatory clause 'for/that'. با تیر او literally 'with his arrow' uses او as a pronoun in the genitive/possessive sense ('his'). گبر is the noun 'infidel' (an enemy here) and comes after the possessive phrase; چون باد بود is a simile 'was like the wind' (چون = like, باد = wind, بود = was — past copula). The overall structure describes the enemy's swiftness or condition as caused/affected by the hero's arrow.
segments.s433-g100: گذر کرده بر کوه پولاد بود is a perfect/periphrastic clause: گذر کرده (literally 'having passed') is the participial construction (گذر + کرده) functioning like 'had passed'; بر + کوه پولاد = 'over/above Mount Pulad' (بر here = over/on), and بود is the past copula 'was' completing the tense. Note پولاد is a proper-name mountain here (hence treated as a name rather than the common noun 'steel').
segments.s433-g101: پس introduces a consequence or transition ('so/then'). The sequence من تو is emphatic classical ordering ('I you' — here functioning as an address or mild emphasis), followed by زود آیی 'come soon/quickly' (آیی = subjunctive/imperative 'come'). ای مهربان is a vocative 'O kind one'. Word order and the vocative are typical of epic/direct speech.
segments.s433-g102: تو از من مرنج و مرنجان روان contains two imperatives paired by و. تو از من مرنج = 'do not be angry with me' (مرنج is negative imperative of رنجیدن 'to be upset'); مرنجان is a causative/second-person imperative 'do not make [others] upset' (the -ان in this context expresses causing), and روان here functions adverbially as 'at ease / calm' or as a closing exhortation. The two imperatives are balanced: do not take offense and do not give offense.
segments.s433-g103: برهنه مکن is a negative imperative 'do not expose/bare' (برهنه = bare/uncovered, مکن = do not do). روی بر انجمن places the object before the locative phrase: روی 'face' + بر انجمن 'before the assembly/assembly hall' (بر = upon/in front of, انجمن = assembly). The whole is a prohibition against showing one's face publicly.
segments.s433-g104: مبین نیز چهر من اندر کفن continues the prohibition: مبین (here from the root بین/بَیْن, used in the sense 'show/reveal' or 'make manifest') is coordinated with the previous negation so the meaning is 'do not show (it) either'. نیز = 'also'; چهر من = 'my face'; اندر کفن = 'in/within the shroud' (اندر = in/within, کفن = shroud). Grammatically, the earlier negative مکن scopes over this clause as well, so the speaker forbids revealing even the speaker's own face, since it is in a shroud.
segments.s433-g113: فرستادم is a first-person past tense verb 'I sent' (classical past). اینک is a temporal adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis. به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'to/near him' (به + نزدیک) indicating destination/position.
segments.s433-g114: که introduces a subordinate/result clause here ('so that' / 'that'). شرم آورد is a third-person past ('brought/caused shame'); آورد has the sense 'cause/bring about' rather than simple reporting. جان تاریک او = 'his dark soul' (noun + adjective + possessive enclitic او).
segments.s433-g115: بگفت is third-person past 'he said' (the prefix ب- is common in Classical Persian narration). این refers to the preceding words. و links the next action. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'struck/fell/occurred' (here 'a pang struck'). یکی تیز دم is a noun phrase 'a certain/sharp moment' (یکی = an indefinite 'one/a certain', تیز = 'sharp', دم = 'moment/pang').
segments.s433-g116: که again links/explains ('for' / 'that'). بر من = 'upon me' (preposition before pronoun for emphasis). ز گشتاسپ آمد ستم is a compressed poetic order: ز = 'from', گشتاسپ = source, آمد = past 'came/happened', ستم = 'oppression/injustice' — literally 'oppression came from Gushtasp'.
segments.s433-g117: هم‌انگه (همان‌گاه) is a temporal adverb 'at that moment'. برفت here is an archaic/perhaps corrupted form meaning 'departed/left' (used of the soul leaving the body); the provided lemma/senses do not match this verbal sense. از تنش = 'from his body' (از + noun + enclitic). جان پاک = 'pure soul' (noun + adjective).
segments.s433-g118: تن خسته is 'weary body' (noun + adjective). افگنده is a past participle/verb meaning 'cast/lying' (here 'was cast' / 'was thrown down'). بر تیره خاک = 'upon the dark earth' (preposition + adjective + noun). The phrase is a poetic description of the corpse lying on the ground.
segments.s433-g119: تهمتن is an epithet/name (Tahmātan, 'the mighty one') used as the subject. بنزد = 'to/at the side of' (to the presence of). پشوتن is the addressee/name (Peshotan). رسید is the past tense 'arrived/came'. This is a straightforward classical S–(PP)–V snapshot: subject + destination + verb.
segments.s433-g120: همه جامه literally 'all the garments' used adverbially ('completely/entirely'). بر تن = 'on (his) body'. سراسر is an intensifier 'throughout/entirely'. درید is past 'tore' (he tore them). Word order places the object phrase before the verb in a compact poetic style.
segments.s433-g73: چو رفتی is a temporal clause ('when you went/when you go') using چو + past 2nd-sg رفتی to mark a time for the main command. پدر را is the definite object/topic marked by را and precedes the verb. بگوی here is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person exhortative/subjunctive/imperative form 'tell' (verb comes after the object), so the clause means 'When you go to Iran, tell (your) father...'.
segments.s433-g74: که introduces the content of what to say (complementizer 'that'). چون in this context is temporal ('when'), followed by کام یابی: یابی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive or generic present meaning 'you attain/reach' (verb agrees with تو). بهانه مجوی is a prohibitive expression: مجوی is a negative jussive/imperative 'do not seek' and the object بهانه ('an excuse') precedes it.
segments.s433-g75: زمانه is the subject (here meaning 'fortune/the times'), سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/entirely', and به کام تو گشت is a predicate: گشت (past of گشتن) 'turned/became', with به کام تو 'into your favor/according to your wishes' as the locative/benefactive phrase. Word order is subject + adverb + predicate.
segments.s433-g76: همه مرزها is the subject phrase 'all the frontiers/borders'. پر ز نام تو is a prepositional/adjectival phrase meaning 'full of your name/reputation' where پر 'full (of)' takes ز ('of') plus نام تو. گشت is the past verb 'became/turned' completing the clause.
segments.s433-g77: امیدم is the noun 'my hope' with the possessive suffix -م. نه این بود literal order 'it was not this' (negation + demonstrative + past of بودن). نزدیک تو is a predicative complement 'your being near' (نزدیک adjective + possessor تو). The clause means 'My hope was not that you be/come near.'
segments.s433-g78: سزا is a nominal predicate here meaning 'the due/deserved (punishment)'. این بد 'this bad/evil (thing)' is its topic, and از جان تاریک تو is an ablative/cause phrase 'of/from your dark soul' (از indicates source/cause; جان تاریک 'dark soul'). The whole line states that this ill is what your dark soul deserves.
segments.s433-g79: جهان is the object/topic 'the world'. راست کردم is a 1st-person past 'I made right/rectified'. به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with the sword'. داد here should be read as the noun 'justice' (or as a short verb phrase 'and gave justice'), completing the idea 'I made the world right and gave justice by the sword.'
segments.s433-g80: به بد کس literally 'for/to a bad person' (بد کس = 'wicked person'). نیارست is an archaic/formal negative copular or adjectival form meaning 'was not fitting/appropriate' (here impersonal), and کرد از تو یاد = 'to make mention/remember you' (کرد is past 'made/did', یاد 'remembrance' with از تو 'of you'). Together: 'it was not fitting for a wicked person to (make) mention of you.'
segments.s433-g25: This is a subordinate relative clause introduced by the conjunction 'که' (that) and the free relative 'هرکس که' (whoever that). The phrase 'او خون اسفندیار' is a poetic word order: 'او' (he/his) precedes 'خون اسفندیار' to indicate 'his (i.e. Esfandiyar's) blood' (the pronoun refers back to Esfandiyar), so the whole unit means 'whoever (that) [sheds] his blood, Esfandiyar's blood.'
segments.s433-g26: The verb 'بریزد' is a subjunctive/present-form verb meaning 'sheds' or 'would shed' (used after 'هرکس که' to state the general condition). 'سرآید' is from 'سرآمدن' meaning 'to come to an end/meet an end' (here indicating the person's fate). The phrase 'برو روزگار' is a compact poetic locution meaning 'in/by fate' or 'in time' (so together: 'will meet his end in time').
segments.s433-g27: The line begins with a poetic split of 'بدین' into prefix 'ب' + 'دین' (here functioning as 'in/with this' — 'in this manner' or 'in this world'); in the provided lemma list 'دین' does not carry its ordinary sense ('religion') and is part of the demonstrative construction, so its listed senses are not used (hence -1). 'گیتیش' is 'گیتی' (world) with a clitic ending, and 'شوربختی بود' is the past copula 'بود' indicating 'there was misfortune.'
segments.s433-g28: 'وگر' is a conditional ('and if' / 'but if'). 'بگذرد' is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'it should/pass' (i.e. 'even if it passes'). 'رنج و سختی بود' uses the past copula 'بود' to state that 'there was sorrow and hardship' — the sentence says that even if misfortune passed, suffering would remain.
segments.s433-g29: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said.' Word order is poetic: 'با رستم اسفندیار' shows the speaker and the addressee — literally 'with Rostam, Esfandiyar' — meaning 'Esfandiyar said this to Rostam.' The preposition 'با' marks the person addressed/engaged with; the verb 'گفت' here is the past 'said' (the listed noun senses do not match the verb form, so -1).
segments.s433-g30: This is reported speech introduced by 'که' (that). 'از تو' literally 'from you' functions in comparative/evaluative sense 'than you' or 'with regard to you.' 'ندیدم' is the 1st-person past negative ('I did not see' / 'I have not seen'). 'بد روزگار' = 'bad fate' (i.e. 'worse fortune'); the whole clause means 'I have not seen a worse fate than yours.'
segments.s433-g31: 'زمانه چنین بود' is a simple past clause 'the age/time was thus.' The repetition 'و بود آنچ بود' is poetic tautology: 'and it was what it was' (آنچ = 'that which'). Each 'بود' is the past copula used for emphasis; word order is emphatic/poetic rather than syntactically unusual.
segments.s433-g32: 'سخن هرچ گویم' = 'whatever words I may say' — 'هرچ' = 'whatever,' and 'گویم' is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I say/should say' (the lemma senses given were for the noun, so treated as -1 here). 'بباید شنود' is a modal construction: 'بباید' = 'it must' / 'ought to' followed by the verb 'شنود' (3rd-person subjunctive/passive sense 'be heard'), so the combined meaning is 'whatever I say must be (or ought to be) heard.'
segments.s433-g49: چنین گفت جاماسپ is a poetic inversion: چنين گفت (= 'thus said') + جاماسپ (the speaker) — literally 'thus said Jamasp.' گم بوده نام is a past-state clause where گم (an adjectival past participle 'lost') + بوده (past auxiliary 'had/was') + نام (the noun ‘name/reputation’). The order adjective–بوده–noun is archaic/poetic and yields 'the name had been lost.'
segments.s433-g50: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the consequence/reason: 'that/so that…'. هرگز is the negator 'never' before the verb. به گیتی is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' (poetic use of به). مبیناد is an archaic 3rd‑person present form meaning 'appears/is seen' and takes کام ('desire/fulfillment') as its semantic subject — 'desire never appears (in the world).'
segments.s433-g51: که links this clause to what precedes ('that/for'). بهمن is the proper name. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (short form of از), so بهمن ز من یادگاری بود = 'Bahman was a keepsake/from me.' یادگاری is the predicate noun 'memento/keepsake' and بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s433-g52: سرافرازتر is the comparative/adjectival form 'more exalted' (–تر comparative). شهریاری is a noun meaning 'sovereignty/royal dignity' or 'sovereign rule.' بود again is the past copula: 'he was a more exalted sovereign.' The adjective precedes the noun here (typical in Persian poetry).
segments.s433-g53: تهمتن is the subject/name. چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = when; بشنید = past 'he heard'). بر پای خاست is an idiomatic verb phrase 'rose to his feet/stood up' (بر + پای + خاست), with خاست the past 'arose.' Word order is subject + temporal clause + predicate, typical in epic style.
segments.s433-g54: This line is compact and slightly inverted for poetic effect. زد is the past 'struck' (main verb). ببر is the noun 'tiger' that functions as the object here (object preceding verb is common in classical Persian). به فرمان او is an adverbial phrase 'by/at his command' modifying the action, and دست راست ('the right hand') names the instrument/agent performing the blow. Literally: 'the right hand, at his command, struck the tiger.'
segments.s433-g55: که introduces a conditional/continuation ('that/if'). تو بگذری is a subordinate conditional 'if you pass/should pass' (بگذری is 2nd‑person subjunctive/present used for the condition). زین سخن = 'over/from this speech' (زین = ز + این, poetic 'from this'). نگذرم is the first‑person negative 'I will not let (it) pass/I will not forgive/overlook' — the negation ن‑ plus verb from گذشتن; together the sense is 'even if you pass by this, I will not let it go.'
segments.s433-g56: سخن هرچ گفتی is a free relative 'whatever words you say' (هرچ = 'whatever'; گفتی is 2nd‑person past form used here to mean 'you say/you may say' in classical usage). به جای آورم is an idiom meaning 'I will bring into place/fulfil/accomplish' — به جای (in place) + آوردن (to bring about). آورم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive used as a future/intention: 'I will carry out.'
segments.s433-g89: ترا is the enclitic combination ت + را (= 'to you' / 'for you'), placed before the noun phrase for emphasis. تخت سختی و کوشش is a coordinated noun phrase (poetically lacking the ezāfe or copula) meaning 'a throne of hardship and of effort/toil.' The final مرا is the contrasting enclitic م + را (= 'to me' / 'for me'), signalling the parallel contrast with ترا; the copula ('is') is omitted (understood).
segments.s433-g90: Again ترا begins the contrasted clause ('for you'), followed by نام تابوت و پوشش — 'the name “coffin” and (a) covering/shroud.' As in the previous segment the copula is omitted and مرا at the end (م + را) marks 'for me,' completing the antithesis between what is for you and what is for me.
segments.s433-g91: چه here is the interrogative 'what.' گفت is the lexical root meaning 'said' (the line treats it as the act of speaking), and آن جهاندیده دهقان پیر is a noun phrase 'that/worldly-wise old farmer' (آن = 'that', جهاندیده = 'worldly-wise', دهقان = 'farmer', پیر = 'old'). The whole segment sets up the question about what this specific man said.
segments.s433-g92: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying the farmer. نگریزد is present-subjunctive/negative form meaning 'does not flee/does not escape' (ن + گریزد). از marks the source or object of fear ('from'), and مرگ پیکان تیر lists the dangers ('death, (the) arrow, (the) spear') — a poetic trio without additional conjunctions beyond juxtaposition.
segments.s433-g93: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become/become not' and combines with ایمن ('safe') to mean 'do not be secure/safe.' از introduces the sources people might rely on ('from/because of'), and the coordinated nouns گنج و تاج و سپاه ('treasure and crown and army') list the things that should not give a false sense of safety.
segments.s433-g94: روانم is روان ('soul/spirit') with the first-person possessive suffix -م = 'my spirit.' ترا (ت + را = 'you') is the object/target. چشم دارد 'has an eye' is an idiom meaning 'keeps watch' or 'keeps an eye on,' and به راه ('on the road' / 'on the way') gives the location or expectation. Word order is subject (my spirit) + object (you) + predicate (watches) + locative phrase.
segments.s433-g95: چو means 'when.' آیی is the second-person present/subjunctive of آمدن 'to come' ('you come'). بهم means 'together' or 'to one another,' پیش + داور = 'before the judge' (پیش = 'in the presence of/before', داور = 'judge'). شویم is first-person plural subjunctive/future-like 'we become' / 'we will be (placed)' — here 'we will appear/stand (before him)'.
segments.s433-g96: بگوییم is first-person plural subjunctive/optative 'we will say / we shall speak' (here 'we will speak'); و = 'and'; گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (گفتار = speech, او = he/his); بشنویم is first-person plural subjunctive 'we will hear' or 'we shall hear.' The clause lays out the actions that will follow when they stand before the judge: we will speak and hear his words.
segments.s433-g41: بمیرم is a first-person optative/exclamatory form literally 'may I die' used here as an oath or emphatic exclamation. پدروارش is پدروار (fatherly, paternal) with the third-person enclitic -ش 'his' = 'his fatherly quality/care'. اندر + پذیر together express acceptance/receiving ('accept as' / 'be acceptable in regard to'); here the phrase functions as an imperative/request: 'accept his fatherliness'. Word order is emphatic: exclamation → object phrase → verb/adjective phrase.
segments.s433-g42: This clause is an imperative instruction: همه هرچ (lit. 'all/whatever') limits the scope; گویم is the first-person verb 'I say/tell' (here used with هرچ 'whatever I say'). ت is the 2nd-person enclitic object 'you' attached as the recipient (often written separately as ترا here). را is the definite/object marker following the clitic; یادگیر is a second-person singular imperative 'learn/remember' (literally 'take into memory'). The literal order is 'All whatever I say — you learn'.
segments.s433-g43: به marks direction or purpose 'to/at'; زابلستان is the place-name 'Zabulistan'. در here denotes the (royal) court/place — 'at the court' — used with ورا ('there'/'to him') to indicate location or where he should be kept. ورا is a demonstrative/pronominal adverb 'there/thither' or 'to him/there'. شاد is an adjective 'happy/joyful'. دار here functions as a verb 'keep/hold' (classical Persian دار = keep/possess), giving the imperative sense 'keep him joyful' (although the supplied dictionary senses were noun-forms).
segments.s433-g44: سخنهای is the plural construct 'words/sayings' (سخن + -های). بدگوی qualifies those words: as an adjective/relational form it means 'ill-speaking' or 'slanderous' (i.e. 'slanderous words' or 'words of a slanderer'). را is the object marker. یاد دار is a compound verb: یاد (memory/recall) + دار (hold) functioning here imperatively 'keep/remember' — 'remember the slanderous words'.
segments.s433-g45: بیاموزش is a third-person jussive/imperative form meaning 'teach him' or 'let him learn' (بی- prefix + آموزش). آرایش کارزار is a genitive-like phrase: آرایش 'array/arrangement' qualifying کارزار 'battle' → 'the arrangement/array of battle' (i.e. battlefield deployment). The verb/phrase orders: 'Teach him the battle-array.'
segments.s433-g46: نشستنگه is a nominal form meaning 'seat/place of sitting' or 'assembly/session' — here 'the seating/assembly' at social occasions. بزم is 'feast/banquet' so نشستنگه بزم = 'the seating/etiquette at feasts'. و is the conjunction 'and'. دشت شکار = 'the plain(s) for hunting' (دشت 'plain' + شکار 'hunt' used as noun 'hunting ground'). The whole segment lists social and field practices to be taught.
segments.s433-g47: This is a list of items: می‌و (here 'wine/fruit' in poetic register) paired with رامش 'tranquility/repose'. و 'and' links items. زخم چوگان = 'wounds of polo' (زخم 'wound' + چوگان 'polo' — injuries sustained playing polo). و links to the next item کار 'deed/affair' which continues into the next segment. The sequence is nominal — items to be taught/known.
segments.s433-g48: بزرگی 'greatness' or 'great deeds' continues the list. و 'and' links. برخوردن is the infinitive/masdar sense 'to befall/occur' (here nominalized: 'encounters/befallings'). از is the preposition 'from' and روزگار in classical Persian can mean 'fate/fortune' or 'the times' — here it means the vicissitudes imposed by fate. Together: 'great deeds and the things that befall from fate.'
segments.s433-g105: ز (preposition) + noun phrase: ز دیدار زاری = “from the sight/seeing of (your) lamentation.” The verb بیفزایدت is classical/poetic: بیفزاید (بَ + افزاید) in third-person singular with the enclitic object -ت “you,” meaning “will add to you / will increase (for you).” Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb as is common in older Persian.
segments.s433-g106: کس از بخردان نیز نستایدت is a negative statement: کس = “no one” here; از بخردان = “of/from the wise (men)” (a partitive/genitive-like construction); نیز = “also/indeed” for emphasis; نستایدت is the present/future negative of ستاید (to praise/approve), with the enclitic -ت “you” → “(they) will not praise you.” The verb is third-person negative, so the subject is implied (“no one will…”).
segments.s433-g107: همان is a demonstrative 'that/those' (here 'those same'); خواهران = plural 'sisters/women'; را marks them as definite/ topical objects. و links the next noun. جفت مرا = 'my jift/partner' (جفت here = 'mate/companion'); the sequence functions as a coordinated noun phrase: 'those same sisters and my companion.'
segments.s433-g108: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; جویا is an adjective/participle 'inquiring, seeking'; بدندی is an older/poetic verbal form (here marking the action of the sisters/companion) and functions as the clause’s verb; نهفت is the past tense of نهفتن 'to hide' and takes مرا ('me') as its object. Overall: 'who inquired / sought (and) hid me.' Note: some tokenization in the line is archaic/irregular, so elements like the enclitic pronoun appear split.
segments.s433-g109: بگویی is a verbal form (classical prefix بَ + گوئی) functioning as an imperative/subjunctive or hortative 'say / tell.' دنبالِ اینچه که follows: بدان here is best parsed as به + آن 'to those/those' (prepositional dative), so the phrase means 'tell that to' or 'say to those.' پرهنر modifies بخردان: 'the cultured/learned wise men.' The whole segment = 'say to those cultured wise men' (i.e., 'tell those cultured wise men').
segments.s433-g110: که introduces the content of what is to be said ('that'); پدرود is a greeting/farewell noun used as a wish; باشید is the present subjunctive/imperative polite second-person plural of بودن 'to be' (here optative: 'may you be'); تا = 'until' (here used idiomatically with جاودان to mean 'for ever'); جاودان = 'eternal.' Together: 'that (you) may be greeted/receive farewell eternally.'
segments.s433-g111: ز تاج پدر = 'from the crown of (my) father' (genitive phrase). بر سرم = 'upon my head' with enclitic -م 'my.' بد رسید is an idiomatic past expression: بد (adverb 'badly/unfavorably') + رسید (past of رسیدن 'to reach/come') → 'ill befell / an unfavorable thing came upon (me).' The clause literally: 'From my father's crown an ill came upon my head.'
segments.s433-g112: در گنج را = here در is best read as 'the door/gate of' (در = 'door, gate') taking گنج 'treasure' as its genitive complement and را as the object/topic marker; جان من شد کلید = 'my life became the key' (شد = past 'became', کلید = 'key'). The syntax is poetic: 'the treasure's gate — my life became its key.'
segments.s433-g17: This is a nominal phrase listing two names/agents in classical order: 'زواره فرامرز' (Zowareh and Faramarz). 'چون' here functions as a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when/since' introducing a circumstance. 'بیهشان' is an archaic compound meaning 'bereft of them / without them' (بیه = 'without' with the 3rd-person plural possessive -شان).
segments.s433-g18: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/withdrew'. چندی modifies the verb and means 'for a while'. ز is the poetic form of 'from'. گردنکشان is the plural noun 'the proud ones / rebels' and is the object/contrastive group they withdraw from.
segments.s433-g19: خروشی برآمد is a simple past/intransitive clause: خروشی 'a shout/roar' as subject and برآمد 'arose/issued forth'. ز آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield' using the poetic ز.
segments.s433-g20: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that' / 'such that'). تاریک شد is a past intransitive 'became dark' (شد = 'became'). روی خورشید و ماه literally 'the face of the sun and the moon' (روی = 'face')—note the classical/genitive relation without an explicit ezāfe marking in this poetic line.
segments.s433-g21: به رستم places the speech's addressee ('to Rostam'). چنین گفت is a fixed reporting phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past of 'to say'). زال‌ای پسر is the vocative: 'Zal — O son' (زال‌ای is an invocation of Zal, and پسر addresses Rostam as 'son').
segments.s433-g22: ترا را is split into the enclitic ت (2nd-person suffix) plus the object marker را, functioning together as 'you' / 'for you' (direct object). بیش گریم is a verbal phrase: بیش 'more' and گریم here has an optative/first-person sense ('may I/weep' or 'I shall weep'), expressing Zal's lament. به درد جگر is an idiomatic complement 'with the pain/anguish of the liver' = 'with heart-ache' (جگر = seat of feeling).
segments.s433-g23: که begins a reported-content clause ('that'). ایدون is an archaic/adverbial element here meaning 'now' or 'recently' (classical usage), and شنیدم is the perfect/past 'I have heard' or 'I heard'. ز دانای چین marks the source: 'from the wise men/sages of China' (ز = 'from', دانای = 'wise').
segments.s433-g24: ز اخترشناسان ایران‌زمین is a further source phrase: ز 'from', اخترشناسان 'the astronomers/astrologers' (plural), ایران‌زمین 'the land of Iran' (a compound denoting the Iranian realm). Together: 'from the astrologers of Iran.'
segments.s433-g57: نشانمش is an archaic/poetic 1st-person verb+object: نشانم (I place/mark) + ـش (object clitic 'it/him/her'), so ‘I will place/affix it’. بر نامور تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'on the famed ivory throne' (بر = on, نامور = famed/renowned, تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). Word order places the verb+clitic before the prepositional location, a common classical Persian arrangement.
segments.s433-g58: The clause begins with the verb نهم (archaic 1sg of نهادن/نِهَدن, 'I put/place'), followed by two prepositional phrases: بر سرش 'upon his head' (سر = head; ـش = his) and بر دلارای تاج 'the dear/beloved crown' (دلارای = beloved, تاج = crown). The doubled بر marks location/placement; overall: 'I place/set ... upon his head the beloved crown.'
segments.s433-g59: ز رستم چو بشنید = 'when Rostam heard': ز (poetic 'from/of'), رستم (proper name), چو (classical 'when'), بشنید (past 'he heard'). گویا سخن follows as a noun phrase modifying what was heard: گویا here is adjectival 'eloquent/apparent' and سخن = 'speech'. So: 'When Rostam heard that eloquent speech...'.
segments.s433-g60: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت is past 'said' used with indirect object). The line that follows is quoted: نوگیر چون شد کهن = 'how/when the new becomes old' (نوگیر 'novel/new' as adjective, چون = 'how/when', شد = 'became', کهن = 'old/ancient'). The clause is a comment on the transience of novelty; نوگیر serves as the topic and the subordinate clause 'چون شد کهن' explains its fate.
segments.s433-g61: چنان دان is an imperative/verb phrase meaning 'consider/know thus' (چنان = thus, دان = consider/know). The subordinate clause introduced by که states the content: یزدان گوای منست = 'God is/my witness' (یزدان = God; گوای here = 'witness' or 'one who testifies'). منست is an archaic copular construction meaning roughly 'is mine / is to me' or 'is so with respect to me' (an older contracted form combining the pronoun and 'ast'); the whole clause reads 'that God is my witness.'
segments.s433-g62: برین دین = 'with regard to this religion/faith' (برین = بر این, 'on/with this'; دین = faith/religion). به رهنمای = 'by/through the guidance (of...)' (به = by/through, رهنمای = guide/guidance). منست repeats the archaic copular idea meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' so the phrase says 'in this faith, the guidance is mine' — i.e. 'I am the guide in this matter.'
segments.s433-g63: کزین نیکویها = 'from these excellences/good deeds' (کزین = از این, 'from/of these'; نیکویها = plural of 'good/excellent things'). که تو کرده‌ای is a relative clause 'which you have done' (که = that; تو = you emphatic; کرده‌ای = perfect 'you have done'). The clause links the listed virtues directly to the addressee's actions.
segments.s433-g64: ز شاهان پیشین = 'of/from the earlier/former kings' (ز = from/of; شاهان = kings; پیشین = former/ancient). که پرورده‌ای is another relative clause 'whom you have reared/nurtured' (پرورده‌ای = perfect 'you have reared'), so the whole segment restricts the previous deeds to those done with respect to the earlier kings you brought up.
segments.s433-g1: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying the speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (verb + past), and با introduces the addressee; the sequence places the speaker's name (اسفندیار) after the verb and the addressee (رستم) with با, so the clause reads 'thus said Esfandiyar to Rostam.'
segments.s433-g2: که introduces the reported content ('that'); اکنون is the time adverb 'now'. سرآمد is used as a noun 'the end/conclusion', and the phrase سرآمد مرا روزگار is a classical idiom meaning 'the end of my days/my fate' (مرا marks possession here and روزگار = fate/time). Together the clause says 'that now the end of my days has come.'
segments.s433-g3: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. اکنون is 'now'. مپرهیز is a negative imperative ('م' negative prefix + پرهیز) meaning 'do not refrain/hold back.' خیز is the imperative 'rise!' and ایدر آی (ایدر = 'here', آی = imperative 'come') together form 'come here.'
segments.s433-g4: که here gives the reason/cause ('for' or 'because'). ما را uses را with ما to mark the group affected ('for us' / 'our'). دگرگونه‌تر is the comparative/adjective 'changed/different', گشت is the past 'became', and رای means 'opinion/counsel' — so the phrase means 'for our counsel/opinion has become different.'
segments.s433-g5: مگر here functions as a conditional 'if'/'unless' introducing a proviso. بشنوی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you hear/listen' (ب + شنوی), and پند و اندرز من is a coordinated noun phrase 'advice and counsel of mine' (من as possessor: 'my').
segments.s433-g6: بدانی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive or optative 'you may/shall know' used as the outcome of the condition. سر مایه is a compound reading as 'chief/capital resource' (سر + مایه), و links 'and', ارز من is 'my value/worth' (ارز = worth, من = my). Grammatically this is the result clause: 'you will know my chief resource and worth.'
segments.s433-g7: بکوشی is a 2nd-person imperative/subjunctive 'make an effort/strive' (ب- prefix + کوشش). و links the verbs. آن را is the direct object 'that (object)'. بجای آوری is a verbal phrase 'bring into the place/restore' (بجای = 'into place', آوری = 'you bring/bring about'), meaning 'bring it back to its proper place.'
segments.s433-g8: بزرگی is the noun 'greatness/glory' that will result. برین here is analyzable as بر + ین (the latter an old/poetic spelling of این) meaning 'upon this'. رهنمای is 'the guide' (object of the preposition), and آوری repeats the verb 'you will bring' — so 'you will bring greatness upon this guide.'
